items.hook: Happy to help you lock this in.
items.bridge: For hotpot, the branch, arrival time, and party size matter more than a quick yes or no.
items.next_step: Ask for branch, time, and number of guests.
items.handoff_suggestion: Route to Catcher if the user replies with reservation facts.

items.hook: That is already enough to start narrowing the next step.
items.bridge: Once the time and headcount are clear, the next question is usually seating type and whether the group needs a milder broth.
items.next_step: Ask whether they need a private room and what spice level fits the table.

items.hook: That is worth choosing a little carefully for a mixed table.
items.bridge: At hotpot, spice tolerance and whether there are children or elders can change the safest broth direction fast.
items.next_step: Ask who is dining and whether they lean spicy, tomato, mushroom, or a milder yin-yang setup.

items.hook: A yin-yang pot usually helps when one table wants two very different heat levels.
items.bridge: The better answer depends on how split the group is and whether the branch supports the exact pot setup you want.
items.next_step: Ask for headcount, spice split, and whether there are children or elders at the table.
items.handoff_suggestion: Good sign of active meal planning.

items.hook: If the meal matters, it makes sense to plan the seating early.
items.bridge: Private room fit usually depends on date, headcount, budget, and whether this is a birthday, family dinner, or team meal.
items.next_step: Ask for dining date, guest count, budget, and whether an invoice or private room matters.

items.hook: Team dinners usually need both the table setup and the service details to line up.
items.bridge: Headcount, invoice needs, private room preference, and arrival time shape the workable plan.
items.next_step: Ask for date, headcount, invoice requirement, and private room need.
items.handoff_suggestion: Route as a high-value banquet lead.

items.hook: I get why you do not want to gamble on a long wait.
items.bridge: Queue time can swing a lot by branch, arrival time, and how many people are coming.
items.next_step: Ask for branch, time, and party size, then guide toward reservation or a safer timing window.
items.handoff_suggestion: Send to a human or booking flow quickly.

items.hook: Happy to point you in a practical direction instead of letting you over-order.
items.bridge: For hotpot, the better picks depend on broth style, spice tolerance, and whether the table leans beef, lamb, seafood, or lighter items.
items.next_step: Ask about broth preference and the party mix.
items.handoff_suggestion: Good engagement signal if the user answers with preferences.

items.hook: A tighter budget can still work if we trim the right part of the order.
items.bridge: At hotpot, broth choice, meat mix, and whether this is everyday eating or hosting all change the range.
items.next_step: Ask for headcount and whether fullness or signature dishes matter more.
items.handoff_suggestion: Workable lead if the user engages on trade-offs.

items.hook: That is exactly the kind of thing worth checking carefully before you order.
items.bridge: Broth, dipping sauce, and kitchen handling can all matter, so it is better to confirm the exact restriction than guess.
items.next_step: Ask for the restriction, the ingredient concern, and the branch.

items.hook: I am glad you asked before coming in.
items.bridge: Allergy questions should be treated as safety issues, not casual flavor preferences.
items.next_step: Ask for the exact allergen and branch, then escalate for confirmation.

items.bridge: With hotpot, people usually care about different proof points like soup depth, spice aroma, meat quality, or whether the dipping setup feels thoughtful.
items.next_step: Invite the user to name the exact flavor or quality point they doubt.

items.hook: Makes sense to check the offer before you make the trip.
items.bridge: Group-buy, member, and holiday rules often change by branch, time slot, and minimum spend.
items.next_step: Ask for the branch and the planned dining time.
items.handoff_suggestion: Useful bridge into booking if the user confirms details.

items.hook: That is worth checking carefully, especially with hotpot ingredients.
items.bridge: Freshness and sourcing questions are easier to answer when the ingredient, branch, and timing are clear.
items.next_step: Ask which ingredient they care about and which branch they are asking about.
items.handoff_suggestion: Route toward branch confirmation if the question stays specific.

items.hook: I can understand why that would feel frustrating.
items.bridge: The fastest way to check a hotpot delivery issue is usually the order time, branch, and a clear photo if there is one.
items.next_step: Move the user to DM for order details and photos.

items.hook: Good call to check the timing before you head over.
items.bridge: Holiday and late-night hours can differ a lot by branch, and queue pressure shifts with the time slot.
items.next_step: Ask which branch and what time they plan to arrive.
items.handoff_suggestion: Booking-ready if the user already has a fixed time.

items.hook: Happy to move faster on this.
items.bridge: The safest first step is still platform DM so reservation or complaint details do not get scattered in public.
items.next_step: Ask for the branch plus booking or order details in DM.
